does: This paper demonstrates that multi-agent LLM deliberation systems on policy questions suffer from artificial consensus, where agents converge on identical recommendations despite assigned diverse value perspectives. The authors introduce the AI Council framework, a three-phase deliberation system, and test two interventions across 120 policy deliberations on child welfare and housing scenarios. Using architectural heterogeneity—assigning different-sized models (7-9B parameters) to represent each value perspective—significantly reduces first-choice concentration (child welfare: 70.9% to 46.1%; housing: 46.0% to 22.9%, both p<0.001). They also implement coherence validation, where a frontier model assesses whether each evaluator's reasoning genuinely reflects its assigned values. The results reveal a fundamental tradeoff between fidelity to values and output diversity, with heterogeneity proving particularly effective on scenarios without dominant objective answers.
connects: This work extends recent research on multi-agent LLM systems for reasoning and decision-making, building on prior studies of agent-based debate and collaborative problem-solving with language models. It responds to earlier findings that homogeneous multi-agent setups often collapse to consensus, previously observed in accuracy-oriented tasks, while exploring whether this pattern persists in normative policy domains lacking ground truth. The paper engages with broader concerns about LLM value alignment and representation of diverse perspectives, connecting to work on value elicitation and pluralistic AI systems. It also relates to research on coherence and interpretability in agent reasoning, using automated validation to check whether agents maintain fidelity to assigned values during deliberation.
enables: This work opens directions for building pluralistic AI systems that genuinely preserve value diversity in high-stakes policy analysis and democratic decision-support tools. The findings suggest architectural design—model assignment based on value perspective—as a practical mechanism for maintaining disagreement, enabling future research into optimal heterogeneity schemes and their scaling properties. The coherence validation approach could extend to other deliberation domains requiring fidelity checks, with potential applications to stakeholder engagement, citizen assemblies, and policy impact assessment tools. The identified fidelity-diversity tradeoff points toward theoretical work on how to preserve productive disagreement while maintaining grounded reasoning, and raises questions about when consensus-seeking versus perspective-preservation should be prioritized in AI-assisted governance.
